responses: Live in Gillette, WY, current residence since 2010.  I moved to Gillette and lived there since June of 1993.  I was born in Bismarck, ND

Ken is the founder and president of Gillette Physical Therapy, P.C., serving the Gillette community since 1993. A Doctor of PT and advocate for healthcare reform, he has focused on improving patient care, advancing standards, and expanding access.
Since 2022, Ken has represented HD 32 in the WY Legislature, serving on the Education Committee and the Labor, Health, and Social Services Committee. He completed the Milbank Program for Emerging Leaders in Healthcare in 2025 and has participated in national policy efforts.
He has led key healthcare legislation, including reforms to credentialing, prior authorization, reimbursement, and accessibility. His work also includes criminal justice, energy, and mental health policy.
Ken previously served on the Wyoming State Board of Education, Campbell County School Board (2022 WY School Board Member of the Year), and Campbell County Healthcare Board. He brings clinical, leadership, and policy experience to public service.

Wyoming’s biggest challenges are diversifying our economy, sustaining communities facing rising costs, and maintaining reliable public services in a rural state. At the same time, we have tremendous opportunities in energy innovation, healthcare delivery, and workforce development. As a legislator, I focus on practical solutions, such as supporting responsible energy production while encouraging emerging sectors, strengthening education and job training pipelines, and improving healthcare access. We must make smart investments that keep communities viable, attract and retain workers, and ensure Wyoming remains a great place to live, work, and raise a family.

Healthcare affordability and access require reducing administrative burdens, increasing provider availability, and improving transparency. I’ve worked on reforms to prior authorization, credentialing, and reimbursement to lower costs and keep providers practicing in WY. We should continue expanding telehealth, support rural clinics, and streamline regulations that drive up costs without improving care. Encouraging competition and innovative care models will help patients get timely, affordable services. My focus is practical reform that keeps care local, reduces delays, and ensures Wyoming citizens receive high-quality care without unnecessary barriers. I am looking at bringing a program from Montana that saved @ 12% on healthcare premiums.

Public lands are central to Wyoming’s economy, culture, and way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer public lands out of public hands. Instead, we should advocate for balanced, responsible management that protects access for recreation, supports grazing and energy development, and preserves natural resources. The state should work collaboratively with federal partners, local stakeholders, and industry to ensure decisions reflect Wyoming values. Maintaining public access, economic use, and long-term stewardship must remain the priority for the benefit of current and future generations.

Property tax relief is important, but it must be balanced carefully with the need to fund essential local services like schools, emergency response, and infrastructure. A one-size-fits-all solution doesn’t work in Wyoming. I support targeted relief that prioritizes homeowners most impacted, while maintaining flexibility for local communities to meet their needs. We should also examine alternative revenue options and efficiencies to reduce reliance on property taxes over time. Thoughtful, balanced policy ensures tax relief without undermining the services Wyoming communities depend on. I have worked on and co-sponsored long-term homeowners' relief, the 4% cap, and have offered other solutions that were community/county specific.

The Legislature plays an important role in creating the conditions for economic growth, not picking winners and losers. Our job is to ensure a stable regulatory environment, invest in workforce development, and support infrastructure that enables businesses to succeed. By fostering innovation, supporting small businesses, and leveraging Wyoming’s strengths, such as energy, natural resources, and rural entrepreneurship, we can build a more resilient economy. Strategic, responsible policy helps attract investment while preserving the values and quality of life that make Wyoming unique. We need to make Wyoming the most business-friendly state, I have worked each year to lower our Workman's Compensation insurance rates and decrease regulations.

Transparency and integrity must guide campaign finance. I support full disclosure so voters understand who supports candidates and why. Wyoming’s system works well in many respects, but there is room to improve clarity, accessibility of reporting, and public understanding. Any reforms should strengthen transparency while encouraging, not discouraging, civic participation.
I am committed to ethical fundraising and to making decisions based on sound policy and the needs of my constituents—not contributions. I was disappointed by the recent “Checkgate” controversy, which was inappropriate and diverted valuable legislative time from addressing the real issues facing Wyoming.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
